payload.content: Section 4.1 first draft complete 2026-05-19. Builds directly on the compiler-density (2026-02-25) and retrieval-budget (2026-05-13) experiments. Both figures + tables in place; narrative wraps 2 days ahead of internal pace target.

Reading-block schedule held: every weekday 09:00-10:30 PT, no meetings, no Slack. Dropped one slot on 2026-05-13 for the experiment run; re-added 2026-05-14. Email and Slack still closed during blocks per habit.

1-line takeaway from Chen 2026: their eval harness is the first published one for multi-session agent recall — directly relevant to our section 4.2. Will adopt their dataset format for our real-task experiments + cite generously.
Imported Chen et al. 2026 'Eval Harnesses for Multi-Session Agent Memory' (arXiv 2605.02338). Tagged: evaluation, multi-session. Reading queue position 4.
Confirmed poster session 4 (Wednesday afternoon). Arushi to fly into Vienna 2026-05-12 morning, present 2026-05-13. Mei joining remotely via the conference Discord.
payload.subject: ICLR 2026 poster slot — Arushi

payload.content: ICLR 2026 poster ('Provenance-Bound Retrieval', accepted 2026-03-26) due to printer 2026-06-15. Arushi started layout in Inkscape 2026-05-14. Mei's lab supplies the foam-board.

Section 4 (experiments) outlined 2026-05-17. Three subsections: 4.1 synthetic-dataset benchmark (compiler density + retrieval budget — both already run), 4.2 real-task evaluation (multi-turn support-agent recall), 4.3 ablation (kind priority off, recency off, semantic off).

Arushi declined a co-author invitation from a former MIT lab member on 2026-05-08 — unrelated paper on tool-use agents, would have pulled focus from NeurIPS. Polite decline citing the 2026-08-22 deadline.

Mei replied 2026-05-15 with detailed comments. Section 3 now looks publication-ready. Figure 4: she suggested adding error bars on the naive-RAG baseline to make the determinism gap more visually obvious. Arushi accepted; figure regen on 2026-05-16.
payload.subject: Re: Section 3 + retrieval-budget figure

payload.content: Sent Mei the revised section 3 plus figure 4 (retrieval-budget sensitivity result). Asked her to review by 2026-05-20 so I can merge feedback before starting section 4 (experimental design).
payload.subject: Section 3 + retrieval-budget figure

payload.content: Reminder set 2026-05-13 for 2026-08-15 NeurIPS abstract deadline (94 days out) and 2026-08-22 full paper (101 days). Internal milestone: full draft to Mei by 2026-07-01 (49 days).

Run completed 2026-05-13. Result: hypothesis confirmed. Statewave deterministic ranking: 67% → 79% → 86% → 89% accuracy across budgets 200/800/2000/4000. Naive RAG baseline: 51% → 73% → 71% → 79% (non-monotonic at the 2000 budget — confirms the determinism failure mode). Going into figure 4 of the paper.

Experiment 'retrieval-budget-sensitivity' designed 2026-05-06: vary max_tokens 200/800/2000/4000 across the 200-episode synthetic dataset. Hypothesis: deterministic ranking should produce monotonic non-decreasing accuracy with budget; non-deterministic baselines should oscillate. Run scheduled for 2026-05-13.

1-line takeaway from Park & Singh 2026: memory-stream scoring (Park 2024) extended to 1M-memory subjects via approximate k-NN; their LLM-grading retrieval-time call costs 4x ours. Cite in section 2 (related work) as an alternative scaling path we explicitly chose against.
Imported Park & Singh 2026 (NeurIPS preprint, arXiv 2604.11827) 'Memory-Stream Scoring at Scale'. Tagged: memory-stream, scoring, generative-agents. Reading queue position 3.

Applied Mei's feedback 2026-05-12: rewrote 3.3 in the abstract — 'an open-source typed-memory implementation' instead of 'Statewave'. Statewave appears in 3.4 only as the eval target. Added bootstrap CI to all headline numbers in 3.4.

60-minute review with Mei. Mei approved 3.1 + 3.2; flagged 3.3 as too implementation-specific (recommended generalising the language so reviewers don't read it as a Statewave ad). 3.4 approved with one note: add 95% CI on the headline numbers.

Section 3 hit 100% on 2026-05-09 — under the mid-May milestone by 6 days. 3.3 reference implementation links Statewave's context.py; 3.4 harness is the eval framework Mei built for the ICLR paper, extended.

Section 3 (methodology) drafted to ~75% on 2026-05-04. Subsections done: 3.1 ranking-formula derivation, 3.2 deterministic-bundle theorem statement (proof in appendix). Pending: 3.3 implementation reference + 3.4 evaluation harness setup.

1-line takeaway from Voigt 2026: their provenance-bound retrieval is a re-derivation of last year's ICLR paper ('Provenance-Bound Retrieval', the Arushi+Mei one) — cite as concurrent work in section 2.
Imported Voigt 2026 'Provenance-Bound Retrieval Revisited' (arXiv 2603.04219). Tagged: retrieval, provenance. Reading queue position 2.

1-line takeaway from Liu 2025: subject-per-user with optional session metadata is empirically the best default for multi-tenant agent memory; subject-per-session devolves to fragmentation in long-running deployments.
Imported Liu et al. 2025 'Subject-Design Patterns for Multi-Tenant Agent Memory' (DOI 10.48550/arXiv.2509.13104). Tagged: subject-design, multi-tenant. Reading queue position 1.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Summary of experimental support for the paper as of May 1: compiler-density (Feb 25) shows LLM extraction is worth its cost on operational data; multi-session-recall (Apr 28) shows structured retrieval beats naïve RAG and history stuffing on temporal tasks; ICLR poster (accepted 2026-03-26) provides earlier supporting work. Section 5 (results) writes itself.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Experiment 'multi-session-recall': 50-session benchmark built per the January hypothesis. Statewave: 0.81 recall@5 on prior-state retrieval; naïve RAG (cosine top-5 over message chunks): 0.34; full history stuffing (no retrieval, just concat): 0.62 but exhausts 8k token budget. Statewave wins on quality + budget; naïve RAG fails on temporal recall.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Personal habit: 90-minute focused reading blocks, two per morning. No meetings before 11:00 PT. Email/Slack closed during reading blocks — review at 11:30.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Section 3 progress: settled on hard cutoff for the formalisation (cleaner proofs of determinism), sigmoid decay relegated to a subsection 'Practical extensions.' Mei reviewed and agrees — async comment on the shared draft.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Open question, still unresolved: should temporal validity be expressed as a hard cutoff (`valid_from`/`valid_to` boundaries flip a memory in/out of the bundle) or a sigmoid decay (memory weight smoothly degrades past `valid_to`)? Hard cutoff is more deterministic; sigmoid decay handles 'mostly stale but still informative' better. Probably need both — model dependent.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Reading queue (in priority order): (1) Liu et al. 2025 on subject-design patterns for multi-tenant agent memory, (2) Voigt 2026 preprint on temporal validity in semantic memory, (3) Tanaka 2025 on idempotent compilation. Plan to finish queue by 2026-04-25 and incorporate citations into section 2.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Insight: ranking determinism implies eval reproducibility — if same subject + same task + same budget always returns the same context bundle, then a regression test on retrieval is well-defined. Vector-only systems can't make that claim because index state varies. This is the core argument for the NeurIPS paper's section 1.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: ICLR 2026 result: 'Provenance-Bound Retrieval' accepted as poster. Reviewer 2 swayed by the vector-baseline comparison; reviewer 3 marked the intro restructure as 'much improved.' Camera-ready due 2026-04-30.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: ICLR 2026 rebuttal pending on the earlier 'Provenance-Bound Retrieval' paper (different work, same vein). Reviewer 2 wanted a comparison against vector-only baselines — added in the rebuttal. Reviewer 3 wanted clearer motivation — restructured intro. Decision expected 2026-03-25.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Key deadline: NeurIPS 2026 abstract due 2026-08-15. Full paper deadline 2026-08-22. Plan: section 3 done by mid-May, full draft to Mei for review by 2026-07-01, four-week revision window before submission.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Experiment 'compiler-density': compared heuristic vs LLM compilation on a 200-episode synthetic operational dataset (shipment events, ticket conversations, billing notes). Result: heuristic extracts ~5 memories per 100 episodes; LLM extracts ~10. Recall@5 on prior-state retrieval: heuristic 0.42, LLM 0.71. LLM ~2× density and ~1.7× recall on operational data.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Notebook style: explicit imports (no `from x import *`), no auto-format on save (autopep8 / black both reflow my list comprehensions in ways that hurt readability). Run formatters only at submission time.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Tooling: Zotero for citations, BibTeX export to the LaTeX source. Obsidian for first-pass note-taking, Markdown drafts converted to LaTeX via pandoc. Don't suggest Mendeley or Notion — they break my pandoc pipeline.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Active draft: 'Scoring Memories with Provenance: A Determinism-First Approach to Agent Memory.' Sections done: 1 (intro), 2 (related work), 4 (architecture). In progress: section 3 (formalisation of the ranking function). Section 3 blocked on whether to express temporal validity as a hard cutoff or a sigmoid decay — undecided.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Read: Chen 2025, 'A Survey of Memory Architectures for Autonomous Agents.' arXiv:2501.04822. Takeaway: surveys 23 systems, taxonomy of episodic/semantic/procedural/working memory. Our framing aligns with their typology. Cite in section 2.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Read: Park et al. 2024, 'Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior.' DOI 10.1145/3586183.3606763. Takeaway: their 'memory stream' uses recency × importance × relevance scoring — same shape as Statewave's ranking but they use LLM scoring at retrieval time (we score at compile time). Useful precedent for the determinism argument.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Collaborator on this thread: Dr. Mei Wu (UC Berkeley). She's sole co-author after the MIT collaborator declined in November. Mei prefers async review — leaves comments in the shared draft, no live calls unless I flag urgent. Timezone: PT.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Working hypothesis: structured, typed memory with deterministic ranking outperforms naïve RAG on multi-session evaluation tasks because (a) provenance enables eval reproducibility and (b) recency × kind-priority weighting handles temporal validity better than pure cosine similarity. To test: build a 50-session benchmark.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Active reading list this quarter: retrieval-augmented generation, mechanistic interpretability, and agent memory architectures. Lit-review scope is post-2022 LLM-era only — anything earlier goes in the historical-context appendix, not the main review.

payload.author: arushi
payload.content: Citation style for shared write-ups: APA 7th edition. Always link the DOI when available. For preprints, link the arXiv abs URL not the PDF. Don't cite blog posts in the formal lit review — separate appendix file for those.
